glossary.definition: deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience; a part of computer science.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020). It is a part of computer science,

glossary.definition: Possessed by humans, animals and even plants; studied in-depth in many fields of science such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc.; psychology has offered theories about its nature and defined characteristics; currently no unified viewpoint if it is a single ability or a set of abilities; problematic to measure due to diversity of people; some tests are static and do not measure individual's ability to learn and adapt over time.
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, it is believed that humans, animals and even plants possess intelligence. Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science... However, it is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence... There is still no a unified viewpoint if intelligence is a single ability or a set of abilities.

glossary.definition: Studied in-depth in many fields of science such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience; psychology has offered theories about its nature and defined characteristics; developed tests allowing assessment of the level of human intellectual development; questioned regarding whether average intelligence for humanity, genius individual, or child with autism spectrum disorder should be adopted as a standard.
glossary.sentence: Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. However, it is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence... what kind of human intelligence should be adopted as a standard in the development of intelligent computer systems: average intelligence for humanity, the intelligence of some genius individual, or the intelligence of a child with autism spectrum disorder?

glossary.definition: Mostly related to learning ability of animals such as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows; more challenging to measure than human intelligence due to greater diversity in cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and ways they explore the environment; not possible to directly explain goals using natural language; studies may be biased expecting it is based on same principles as human intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence. It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence... Moreover, it is not possible to directly explain to the animal what goal it should achieve as we can do in case of humans using our natural language.

glossary.definition: Not based on a brain and nervous system; possesses forms of intellectual performance including learning based on repeated practice, memory, ability to perceive aspects like shape, colour, smell and sound simultaneously, sense of place and awareness of neighbourhood; continually gathers and updates diverse information about environment, integrates with internal state, makes decisions reconciling well-being with environment.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge about plant intelligence is even more limited. It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system... plants possess many forms of intellectual performance, such as they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time.

glossary.definition: A field of science that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence; developed a set of tests allowing assessment of the level of human intellectual development; witnessed a crisis in the quest for what human intelligence is.
glossary.sentence: However, it is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence. Moreover, this field of science has also developed a set of tests allowing assessment of the level of human intellectual development. At the same time, psychology has to some extent witnessed a crisis in the quest for what human intelligence is.

glossary.term: Artificial Intelligence Definition by John McCarthy
glossary.definition: Proposed in 1956; defines artificial intelligence by emphasizing its similarity to human intelligence; states that every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be precisely described so a machine can simulate it; involves finding how to make machines use language, form abstractions and concepts, solve kinds of problems now reserved for humans, and improve themselves.
glossary.sentence: The first definition of artificial intelligence was offered by John McCarthy in 1956... The definition proposed by McCarthy defines artificial intelligence by emphasizing its similarity to human intelligence, and so it is the following: 'The study is to proceed on the basis of the conjecture that every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it.'

glossary.definition: Underlies systems that think humanly; considers human being to be an information processing system; research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.

glossary.definition: Based on logics; knowledge is represented as logical rules (e.g., if 'All students have a student ID' and 'Alex is a student', then conclude 'Alex has a student ID'); allows inference of new knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics. As a result, knowledge is represented as logical rules... than we can conclude (to infer new knowledge) that 'Alex has a student ID'.

glossary.definition: An approach underlying systems that act humanly.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: One of two standards of intelligent performance; used in definitions on the right side of Russell and Norvig's categories; adopted as an ideal intelligence standard.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the categories use two standards of intelligent performance... the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard.

glossary.definition: Categories at the top in Russell and Norvig's grouping; include definitions related to thought processes and reasoning.
glossary.sentence: The previously mentioned categories of definitions focus on different aspects of intelligent performance... the categories at the top include definitions related to thought processes and reasoning.

glossary.definition: Categories at the bottom in Russell and Norvig's grouping; encompass definitions related to behaviour.
glossary.sentence: The previously mentioned categories of definitions focus on different aspects of intelligent performance... the categories at the bottom encompass definitions related to behaviour.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems where symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable and it have a very good sense of reasoning for the solved problem.
glossary.sentence: There are several approaches to artificial intelligence , based on comparing the capabilities of artificial intelligence systems with those of humans. Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) where connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable, but it solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution.
glossary.sentence: There are several approaches to artificial intelligence , based on comparing the capabilities of artificial intelligence systems with those of humans. Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.

glossary.definition: A hypothesis defined by Newell and Simon in 1976 which states that a physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action.
glossary.sentence: It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: 'A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action' (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: A system capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Elements that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes: symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality

glossary.definition: Structures consisting of symbols that are connected to each other in some way, which depict important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain;

glossary.definition: Constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying processes which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures;

glossary.definition: Intelligent performance achieved through a physical symbol system having the necessary and sufficient means for it.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: Systems being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or to cope with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: Knowledge consisting mainly of generalisations that are available in the relevant problem domain.
glossary.sentence: This knowledge consists mainly of generalisations that are available in the relevant problem domain.

glossary.definition: Concepts closely related to deep learning that have found application in various fields including text classification, student performance modelling, character recognition, credit card attrition, video surveillance, stock market prediction, cancer cell analysis, and vehicle scheduling; they can be used for both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, artificial neural networks are experiencing their heyday. Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks have found application in various fields: text classification and categorization, student performance modelling, character recognition, credit card attrition, video surveillance, stock market prediction, cancer cell analysis, vehicle scheduling, etc. Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.

glossary.definition: also called processing units or nodes.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: Values attached to the connections between artificial neurons, reflecting the strength of these connections.
glossary.sentence: numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: A distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols; knowledge is encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections.
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, there is an opposite view of the concept of knowledge: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: An example of criticism described by John Searle where a person manipulates Chinese symbols correctly without understanding them, showing that physical symbol manipulation alone does not imply intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle's Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: A field of practical and theoretical research that relies on the physical symbol system hypothesis.
glossary.sentence: Despite criticisms, the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A field of practical and theoretical research that relies on the physical symbol system hypothesis.

glossary.definition: A domain distinct from the real world where computational objects reside.
glossary.sentence: However, this is not a trivial task, because to develop such systems we need to make a transition from the real world to the world of intelligent computer systems .

glossary.definition: A way of structural organization of data about the real world.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world;

glossary.definition: Places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others.
glossary.sentence: data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others;

glossary.definition: A process in which playing a game can be seen as, involving changing between game states by making legal moves.
glossary.sentence: Playing a game can be seen as a problem-solving process.

glossary.term: initial state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: A state we are in at present.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process )

glossary.term: goal state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: A state we would like to reach.
glossary.sentence: a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process )

glossary.definition: The complete or partial graph representing all possible states in a game; it can be processed using minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: A set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: single neurons in artificial neural networks, also called processing units.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: The topmost node in the game tree; it has no ancestor.
glossary.sentence: However, there is no ancestor for the root node, and the algorithm therefore goes down again, via the first unconsidered path.

glossary.definition: A node that corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process; from the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: Nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process; from the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: Connections between nodes in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph, we have only nodes and the arcs connecting them.

glossary.definition: The change from one state to another state taking place by carrying out some action.
glossary.sentence: The transition from one state to another state takes place by carrying out some action.

glossary.definition: States that are passed through to get from an initial state of the problem-solving process to a goal state.
glossary.sentence: This structure indicates that you need to pass through a series of intermediate states to get from an initial state of the problem-solving process to a goal state.

glossary.definition: The starting point of the problem-solving process, such as a blank grid when starting tic-tac-toe, or the root node containing initial parameters like numerical string and scores.
glossary.sentence: In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid... The root node contains the initial numerical string and a score of 8 for each player.

glossary.definition: States in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (in tic-tac-toe), representing a condition to reach to win.
glossary.sentence: Each player wants to reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row.

glossary.definition: Discrete states of the game that change as moves are made; they appear at different levels in a state space graph and represent the condition of the game at specific moments.
glossary.sentence: Thus, playing a game involves changing between game states by making legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: Levels in a state space graph where one or more nodes are located, displaying states from which only one of the two players can make a move; levels distinguish turns based on alternating moves.
glossary.sentence: node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph. One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.

glossary.term: deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where moves are made alternately, node levels are distinguishable based on whose turn it is, and restrictions apply to arc drawing (no arcs between same-level nodes).
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information... which would contradict the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information as defined at the beginning of this topic.

glossary.definition: Properties related to a computer implementation of a game and its course, with values that change after making a move; they characterize states in the game and are represented in each node of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Before creating a state space graph, the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game... they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game;

glossary.definition: Goal states of a game represented as leaf nodes in a complete state space graph; also refers to terminal nodes at specific depths in partial graphs.
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph includes all states... to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes). In the case of a partial state space graph, a search-based game algorithm is applied to only a sub-graph... root and leaf nodes are mostly unavailable. Instead of these nodes, the algorithm processes... terminal nodes of the sub-graph at the specified depth.

glossary.definition: A process that sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph to find a solution path, which may result in situations determining how fast and whether it will find a solution.
glossary.sentence: The search principles explained previously have shown that while searching for a solution path, the search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph. This process may result in several situations that determine how fast and whether the search algorithm will find a solution path.

glossary.definition: A process related to how humans solve problems and how it is represented in an intelligent computer system using a state space graph, involving choosing appropriate alternatives from all possible ones.
glossary.sentence: Topic 2.3 - State Space Search The last topics have explained how humans solve problems and how a problem-solving process is represented in an intelligent computer system by using a state space graph.

glossary.definition: Nodes at the same level that have the same values for all parameters characterizing states of the game; they are displayed only once per level to save computational resources.
glossary.sentence: Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game. If there are identical nodes at the same level, they are not displayed several times, but only once per level...

glossary.definition: Resources required to store and process a state space graph, influencing whether a complete or partial graph is used.
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph is typically used in simple games with a small number of states, or when sufficient computational resources are available... A partial state space graph is normally used for complex games with a huge number of states, or when there are insufficient computational resources to store and process the state space graph during the operation of the intelligent computer system...

glossary.definition: The initial node from which the search begins, added to the OPEN list in iteration 0.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the algorithm does not backtrack in case of failure. Let us consider an example of Beam Search... The search start node is A.

glossary.definition: The target node that the algorithm aims to reach; if found or if leaf nodes with better heuristic measures are reached, the algorithm stops.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the heuristic measure of the search goal node are reached.

glossary.definition: The output provided by the search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: The algorithms provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; they are nodes the search algorithm has already generated but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: A search process that starts from a root node and proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph, moving from given data of a problem toward a goal. The search start node is selected from the set of root nodes, but the search goal node is selected from the set of leaf nodes.
glossary.sentence: From the given data of a problem toward a goal. This is called data-driven search or forward chaining . The search process starts from a root node and it proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A search process that starts from a leaf node and proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph, moving from a goal back to the data. The search start node is selected from the set of leaf nodes, but the search goal node is selected from the set of root nodes.
glossary.sentence: From a goal back to the data. This is called goal-driven search or backward chaining . The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: An ordered sequence of actions (e.g., move left-right-straight ahead) derived from a solution path, where graph arcs represent actions of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: One of the solution paths is C1->B1->B2->B3, or, taking into account that graph arcs represent actions of the problem-solving process, we get a robot motion trajectory - the robot should move left-right-straight ahead.

glossary.definition: A choice influenced by factors such as availability of data about the problem, easiness of defining a search goal, and the structure of the state space graph; early selection can eliminate branches.
glossary.sentence: To select a search direction, it is necessary to analyse a problem by taking the following factors into account: availability of data about the problem; easiness of defining a search goal; the structure of the state space graph. Early selection of a goal can eliminate most of these branches.

glossary.term: Measurable/Comparable Property of Search Goal Node
glossary.definition: A method to specify what exactly the search algorithm needs to reach by making a simple comparison between the content of the current node and the search goal node (e.g., comparing locations).
glossary.sentence: Using a measurable/comparable property of the search goal node. In this case, the search algorithm makes a simple comparison between the content of the current node and the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A method to specify what exactly the search algorithm needs to reach by searching for specific path characteristics such as the shortest path, longest path, or a path visiting each node only once (e.g., based on time, length, resource consumption).
glossary.sentence: Checking a property of the solution path, for example, by searching for the shortest path, the longest path, or a path visiting each node only once.

glossary.definition: Situations where data-driven search is preferred: when all or most data are given in the initial problem statement, or when it is difficult to formulate a goal (e.g., medical diagnosis based on symptoms).
glossary.sentence: Data-driven search is preferred in the following cases (Luger, 2009) : All or most of the data are given in the initial problem statement... It is difficult to formulate a goal.

glossary.definition: Situations where goal-driven search is suggested: when a goal is given in the problem statement or it can easily be formulated.
glossary.sentence: Goal-driven search is suggested if (Luger, 2009) : A goal is given in the problem statement or it can easily be formulated.

glossary.definition: Data that is not given but must be acquired by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Problem data are not given but must be acquired by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A proposed explanation about a potential diagnosis developed by a doctor after a patient describes symptoms, which can be confirmed or denied using diagnostic tests.
glossary.sentence: In medical diagnosis, after the patient describes his/her symptoms, the doctor develops a hypothesis about the potential diagnosis. Taking into account that there is a variety of diagnostic tests that can be applied, the doctor orders only those that are necessary to confirm or deny a hypothesis about the patient's diagnosis (Figure 13).

glossary.definition: A situation in a state space graph where nodes are connected such that an algorithm can get stuck and never find the goal (e.g., H-D-C-H); algorithms should prevent entering cycles even if allowed.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node. Thus it would be logical to prevent the search algorithm from entering cycles, even though they are allowed in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: a mechanism not implemented by the Hill Climbing algorithm; the search stops when a node is found whose heuristic measure is better than its descendants/ancestors.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm does not implement backtracking. The search stops when: the search goal node is reached or when a node whose heuristic measure is better than the measures of its descendants/ancestors is found.

glossary.definition: A high-level strategy whose ideas are used by other search algorithms to find solutions more effectively than basic backtracking.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: A list needed to support backtracking.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking introduces several practical aspects related to the implementation of other search algorithms... a list of nodes awaiting expansion is needed to support backtracking;

glossary.definition: algorithms that receive the search start node and search goal node as input, use a state space graph data structure, and provide a solution path as output.
glossary.sentence: In Module 2 of our course, we considered the operational steps of uninformed search algorithms. They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: algorithms that differ from uninformed search by receiving an additional input (principles for calculating heuristic measures) and performing two additional steps: evaluating generated nodes using a heuristic evaluation function and selecting/ordering nodes according to heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform. Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: An algorithm guaranteed to find the search goal node if it exists in the state space graph because this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the state space graph, but is not guaranteed to find the shortest path.
glossary.sentence: Depth-First Search is guaranteed to find the search goal node if it exists in the state space graph because this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the state space graph. However, this algorithm is not guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node, if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback related to going along indefinitely deep paths by using a defined depth limit.
glossary.sentence: To overcome this shortcoming, a modification of Depth-First Search is offered. Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21). Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions. It uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The algorithm includes an additional operational step where, after expanding the current nodes, it checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that chooses a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, or chooses a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure.
glossary.sentence: Thus, if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, then Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A comparison algorithm mentioned in relation to Beam Search speed.
glossary.sentence: Although this algorithm may lead to the search goal node faster than Best-First Search, there are not any guidelines on how the beam width should be chosen.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that maintains an OPEN list containing a number of nodes equal to the beam width, does not backtrack on failure, and uses heuristic measures to select the best nodes.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Beam Search by implementing data-driven search on the state space graph... Therefore, the algorithm does not backtrack in case of failure.

glossary.definition: A system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.

glossary.definition: Algorithms used to demonstrate the difference between search algorithms using some of the roads between the cities that can be used.
glossary.sentence: In Figure 2, you can see some of the roads between the cities that can be used to demonstrate the difference between search algorithms.

glossary.definition: In the context of a state space graph, each node represents a settlement.
glossary.sentence: These roads can be easily represented as a state space graph, where each node represents a settlement, and each arc represents an actual road between two settlements.

glossary.definition: In the context of a state space graph, each arc represents an actual road between two settlements.
glossary.sentence: These roads can be easily represented as a state space graph, where each node represents a settlement, and each arc represents an actual road between two settlements.

glossary.definition: A type of search where all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.

glossary.definition: Three main steps performed by any search algorithm: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node.
glossary.sentence: In the previous topic of the course, you have already considered that any search algorithm performs three main steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node. Uninformed search algorithms rarely include other steps.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to store a set of elements in sequential order, often used for implementing search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are often implemented using a data structure called a list. A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list that always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding iteration 0 when only the search start node is added to it and cases where the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes that have been expanded or generated but are not selected as the best nodes.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far. The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node. The node D is also moved to the CLOSED list as a generated node, but it is not taken as the best node.

glossary.definition: The first column of a table used to represent the OPEN and CLOSED lists, displaying the iteration number.
glossary.sentence: To represent these lists in our course, we will use a table consisting of three columns (Figure 4). The first column displays the iteration number, the second column displays the OPEN list, and the third column displays the CLOSED list.

glossary.definition: The node located on the first position in the OPEN list that usually becomes the next current node for processing.
glossary.sentence: A node located on the first position in the OPEN list usually becomes the next current node.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure where new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list in Depth-First Search, working according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A terminal node in the game tree where a precise heuristic measure can be assigned (e.g., −1 or 1); at a leaf node, the minimiser may have the highest score and win the game.
glossary.sentence: It reaches a leaf node in which the minimiser has the highest score and therefore wins the game.

glossary.definition: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G.

glossary.definition: A serious shortcoming where Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.
glossary.sentence: Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths . It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: A parameter defined before the algorithm starts its operation that indicates how deeply the state space graph can be explored.
glossary.sentence: The title of the algorithm reveals its main idea (Figure 10): “depth” means to go deeper and deeper into the state space graph while expanding nodes; “limited” indicates that there is a depth limit that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored. The depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation.

glossary.definition: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: Labels added with nodes during implementation that allow the algorithm 'to understand' if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of the algorithm, the depth limit should be defined, and nodes should be added to both lists together with their depth labels. Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: The depth of a node, where the start node's depth level is equal to 0 and any other node's depth level is calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node.
glossary.sentence: The depth level of the search start node is equal to 0. The depth level of any other node is calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node.

glossary.definition: The initial step of the algorithm where only the search start node is added to the OPEN list.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list... Iteration 0: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: An iteration where the algorithm expands the current node by generating its three descendants.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: An iteration where the algorithm expands the current node D by generating its descendant - the node J.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 2: The algorithm expands the current node D by generating its descendant - the node J.

glossary.definition: An iteration where the algorithm moves a node to the CLOSED list and selects another current node from the OPEN list.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 3: The algorithm moves the node J to the CLOSED list. The node H, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: An iteration where the algorithm expands the current node H by generating its three descendants - nodes J, I, and E.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 4: The algorithm expands the current node H by generating its three descendants - nodes J, I, and E.

glossary.definition: An iteration where the algorithm expands the current node E by generating its three descendants - nodes I, K, and F.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 6: The algorithm expands the current node E by generating its three descendants - nodes I, K, and F.

glossary.definition: A figure that reveals the main idea of Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: The title of the algorithm reveals its main idea (Figure 10): “depth” means to go deeper and deeper into the state space graph while expanding nodes; “limited” indicates that there is a depth limit that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored.

glossary.definition: A figure used for an analogy with the tree crown to make evident the difference between Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: The analogy with the tree crown used before makes evident the difference between Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: A figure illustrating that in each iteration, the algorithm performs two checks.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search includes an additional operational step. In each iteration, the algorithm performs two checks (Figure 12): it checks if the current node is the search goal node and, if not, it checks if the depth of the current node is lower than the specified depth limit.

glossary.definition: A figure showing a state space graph used as an example for Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Depth-Limited Search by implementing data-driven search in the state space graph you see in Figure 13.

glossary.definition: An element used in an analogy to explain the difference between search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21).

glossary.definition: Lists used in Bidirectional Search where nodes are added before being expanded; the algorithm checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: Lists used in Bidirectional Search where nodes are moved after being expanded; initially empty because none of the nodes has been expanded yet.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: Nodes located on the first positions of the OPEN lists that the algorithm checks to see if they are the search goal node or start node.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located on the first positions of the OPEN lists become the current nodes.

glossary.definition: A path found by Bidirectional Search, which is able to find this to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, it is also able to find the shortest path to the search goal node.

glossary.term: average numbers of connections per node
glossary.definition: A factor that can cause Bidirectional Search to be unable to find a point of contact of the two search directions if they are very different in both directions.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, sometimes this algorithm is not able to find a point of contact of the two search directions due to very different average numbers of connections per node in both directions.

glossary.definition: The meeting point between data-driven search and goal-driven search that Bidirectional Search looks for.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: knowledge used in intelligent computer systems to guide search processes and reduce complexity by visiting fewer nodes.
glossary.sentence: I hope that you now know a lot about heuristic knowledge and its application in intelligent computer systems, and we can move to the implementation aspects of heuristic search.

glossary.definition: A widely used concept corresponding to what is understood by heuristic knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Today, the widely used concept of “life hack” corresponds to what is understood by heuristic knowledge.

glossary.definition: Strategies people use to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way.
glossary.sentence: Taking into account that a problem can have a large number of solutions, people use strategies (heuristic methods) to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way.

glossary.definition: A description of heuristic knowledge which will not give the perfect outcome, but it will nevertheless usually lead to a good or at least acceptable outcome with much less effort.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is a rule of thumb: it will not give the perfect outcome, but it will nevertheless usually lead to a good or at least acceptable outcome with much less effort.

glossary.definition: applied and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes.
glossary.sentence: It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes.

glossary.definition: The amount of effort needed for the in-depth analysis of a problem, which heuristic knowledge reduces.
glossary.sentence: It reduces the cognitive workload that would be needed for the in-depth analysis of a problem.

glossary.definition: propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: An abstract representation of the heuristic evaluation function where f(n) = g(n) + h(n).
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n)

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available.
glossary.sentence: g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure characterizing the quality of node n or an estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node, allowing the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: A representation form for the heuristic evaluation function used in practical applications, focusing on factors characterizing states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: The weight of the ith factor in the polynomial representation of the heuristic evaluation function.
glossary.sentence: ai – the weight of the ith factor

glossary.definition: The heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A game used as an example for building an intelligent computer system with a state space graph, where nodes are evaluated based on factors like potentially winning positions.
glossary.sentence: Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe. The system has a state space graph of the game.

glossary.definition: A puzzle involving 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares, where players move a tile onto an empty square to achieve a goal state, often evaluated by factors like tiles out of place or distances from intended places.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares. Players are allowed to move a tile onto an empty square with the aim of achieving the goal state

glossary.definition: Knowledge used to solve problems that depends on the nature of the problem, such as game strategies or financial management rules.
glossary.sentence: To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience. This knowledge depends on the nature of the problem.

glossary.definition: Nodes generated from a current node in the search process within a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that in the search process, the algorithm has generated three descendants of the current node C (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: The measure of how promising or better a node is compared to others, determined by factors like the possibility of reaching the search goal and path length.
glossary.sentence: How can we evaluate that one node, for example, E, is better or more qualitative than others... The quality of nodes is measured by two factors: the possibility of reaching the search goal node from the given node n

glossary.definition: Uncertainties in the problem statement or available data that may prevent a problem from having an exact solution.
glossary.sentence: It is used for solving a) problems that may not have an exact solution because of inherent ambiguities in the problem statement or available data

glossary.term: Fallible application of human heuristic knowledge
glossary.definition: The drawback where the application of human heuristic knowledge could be fallible and may not give a solution due to different individual experiences.
glossary.sentence: What drawbacks does it have? The application of human heuristic knowledge could be fallible and may not give a solution, because every individual has different experiences, and rules based on heuristic knowledge are inherently individual

glossary.definition: A type of outcome from the application of heuristic evaluation function where the result is satisfactory but not necessarily the best.
glossary.sentence: The application of heuristic evaluation function can lead to the best solution but can also lead to only an acceptable solution or to no solution at all

glossary.definition: A possible negative outcome from the application of a heuristic evaluation function where no solution is found.
glossary.sentence: The application of heuristic evaluation function can lead to the best solution but can also lead to only an acceptable solution or to no solution at all

glossary.definition: a property used in games like Tic-tac-toe to consider only three distinct states (corner, centre, and side) at the first level of the game tree because it does not matter in which corner a sign is placed.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12). At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.

glossary.definition: a structure representing possible states in a game; using symmetry features can reduce the number of nodes from 362,880 to 60,480.
glossary.sentence: This allows the reduction of the number of states in the game tree from 362,880 (if all nodes are represented) to 12*7! = 60,480 nodes.

glossary.definition: a heuristic search algorithm where the idea is to always move to the best node in the state space graph, climbing up to the best heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. In this case, the Hill Climbing algorithm will always climb up to the best heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: the main actions performed by search algorithms: selecting the current node; checking if it is the goal; expanding it if not.
glossary.sentence: They have three main operational steps (Figure 1): selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A type of search implemented on a state space graph as exemplified in Beam Search.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Beam Search by implementing data-driven search on the state space graph that you can see in Figure 9.

glossary.definition: Nodes generated from the current node depending on the search direction; descendants are generated when expanding a node, while ancestors may be relevant depending on the direction.
glossary.sentence: ...depending on the search direction) of the current node... generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: the cost balance between the number of factors in a function and the effort required to calculate its values.
glossary.sentence: However, in this case, it is necessary to remember the cost balance between the number of factors included in the function and the computational costs of calculating the values of the function.

glossary.definition: a system that requires knowledge to solve problems, explain solutions, maintain communication with users, and is often developed based on human intelligence standards.
glossary.sentence: In general, 'knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems'... This topic will explain how to equip an intelligent computer system with knowledge.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: ...the most common of these shortcomings are the following: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction);

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: ...the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration while keeping all nodes found before, allowing it to switch between different paths in the state space graph and not lose a problem solution if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before. This allows the algorithm to switch between different paths in the state space graph, and it does not lose a problem solution if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to implement the OPEN list in Best-First Search, where the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first based on ordering from a node with the best heuristic measure to the node with the worst.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue. The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: A value indicating the outcome for a player (e.g., that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher); it can be precise or intermediate.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: A parameter that determines the maximum number of nodes kept in the OPEN list at any given time during Beam Search.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width... According to the defined beam width, the algorithm selects the two best nodes based on the heuristic measures.

glossary.definition: A graph that includes all states, from the initial state of the game (root node) to all goal states (leaf nodes), typically used in simple games or when computational resources are sufficient.
glossary.sentence: Definition A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A more complex case where the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: A type of game where the outcome depends on player actions with no chance elements, one player's gain equals another's loss, and it is played by two players.
glossary.sentence: The selection of an algorithm for the implementation of a deterministic two-player zero-sum game of perfect information depends on the availability of the state space graph...

glossary.definition: A property of games where all relevant information about the game state is available to both players at all times.
glossary.sentence: The selection of an algorithm for the implementation of a deterministic two-player zero-sum game of perfect information depends on the availability of the state space graph...

glossary.definition: A player whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: A player whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case the minimiser will take the measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure will be lower, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the maximiser operates; at this level, the algorithm assigns measures based on known heuristic measures of direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MAX level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the minimiser operates; at this level, the algorithm assigns measures based on known heuristic measures of direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: located at the predefined depth.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth.

glossary.definition: The process involving representing games using state space graphs and equipping intelligent computer systems with algorithms to play them.
glossary.sentence: Topic 3.3 - Heuristic Search Algorithms for Game Development To implement a game... we also need to equip an intelligent computer system with an algorithm that will process the graph, with the aim of imitating game playing and striving to win the game (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: Rules of a game that can be formalized to generate a state space graph during operation.
glossary.sentence: ...if the state-transition rules of the game can easily be formalised.

glossary.definition: An algorithm applied to a state space graph (complete or partial) to determine results or select moves.
glossary.sentence: Application of a search-based game algorithm In the case of a complete state space graph, a search-based game algorithm can be applied to the whole graph... In the case of a partial state space graph, a search-based game algorithm is applied to only a sub-graph of the complete search state space graph.

glossary.definition: The final heuristic measure assigned to a node that represents the outcome of the game (e.g., −1, 0, or 1); it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MAX level, and thus acquires the maximum heuristic measure from its direct descendants. This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far, or based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants when there are no other unconsidered descendants.
glossary.sentence: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: A value assigned when it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure; for example, an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1 indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher.
glossary.sentence: An intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A path below a current node that is not yet evaluated and may be cut off during an α-cut-off or β-cut-off.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A node above the current node in the current path; its heuristic measure (α value) is compared with the intermediate heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned this intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path has also an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A procedure performed when the value of β is smaller than α, involving cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: The measure of the current node used for comparison; when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node. When the value of β is smaller than α (the α value), an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The measure of the ancestor used for comparison; when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node. When the value of β is smaller than α (the α value), an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The action performed by the algorithm to stop evaluating unconsidered paths below a current node when an α-cut-off condition is met.
glossary.sentence: ...meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node. The algorithm cuts off one unconsidered path... After the cut off is performed, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: A child or lower-level node connected to a current node, whose heuristic measures are used to determine the value of the parent node.
glossary.sentence: ...since the node at this level do not have other descendants. ... The descendants of the current node have heuristic measures of 1 and −1. ... assigns a precise heuristic measure based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants.

glossary.definition: The action where the algorithm moves a heuristic measure up one level along the same path used to go down and tries to assign it to other nodes.
glossary.sentence: When a precise heuristic measure has been assigned, the algorithm goes up one level, along the same path that was used to go down. It then tries to propagate this heuristic measure to other nodes.

glossary.definition: A label or identifier for a node, exemplified by the string "2-3-3" mentioned in the text as belonging to a node with an unconsidered descendant.
glossary.sentence: ...finds that the node with the numerical string "2-3-3" has another unconsidered descendant.

glossary.definition: A procedure performed when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, involving cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: Paths identified using game results; they are the same as for the minimax algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Using the game results, winning paths can be identified (Figure 9). These are the same as for the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: rules used for propagating heuristic measures up to the current node.
glossary.sentence: Acquired heuristic measures are propagated up to the current node using the rules of the minimax algorithm or alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: N-ply look-ahead explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.

glossary.definition: explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009).

glossary.definition: principles used for propagating heuristic measures back up to the node of the current state.
glossary.sentence: Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: one of the most significant problems with this approach.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game.

glossary.definition: may be so huge that the time limit could expire before it can be analysed.
glossary.sentence: In this situation, it is not possible to use N-ply look-ahead, since a sub-graph may be so huge that the time limit could expire before it can be analysed.

glossary.definition: a highly topical area in the field of computer science; a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building; a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention; involves adaptive mechanisms that enable computers to learn from experience, learn by example and learn by analogy.
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, machine learning is a highly topical area in the field of computer science. ... Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.” In general, machine learning involves adaptive mechanisms that enable computers to learn from experience, learn by example and learn by analogy.

glossary.definition: one of the keywords giving insight about the nature of machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Although the definitions provided above have already introduced some keywords giving insight about the nature of machine learning (i. e. experience, analytical model building, learning from data, identifying patterns, improving the performance), to get a better understanding of the concept of machine learning, we need to pay attention to three aspects.

glossary.sentence: Although the definitions provided above have already introduced some keywords giving insight about the nature of machine learning (i. e. experience, analytical model building, learning from data, identifying patterns, improving the performance), to get a better understanding of the concept of machine learning, we need to pay attention to three aspects.
