glossary.definition: one of the keywords giving insight about the nature of machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Although the definitions provided above have already introduced some keywords giving insight about the nature of machine learning (i. e. experience, analytical model building, learning from data, identifying patterns, improving the performance), to get a better understanding of the concept of machine learning, we need to pay attention to three aspects.

glossary.definition: one of the keywords giving insight about the nature of machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Although the definitions provided above have already introduced some keywords giving insight about the nature of machine learning (i. e. experience, analytical model building, learning from data, identifying patterns, improving the performance), to get a better understanding of the concept of machine learning, we need to pay attention to three aspects.

glossary.definition: the broadest concept in this context; the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, computer science is the broadest concept in this context. It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: robotics, evolutionary computations, intelligent agents, knowledge representation, and others.
glossary.sentence: It is a part of computer science, and it also consists of many sub-fields such as robotics, evolutionary computations, intelligent agents, knowledge representation, and others.

glossary.definition: one of the three types of algorithms included in machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence. It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: one of the three types of algorithms included in machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence. It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: one of the three types of algorithms included in machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence. It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: allows it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space; statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics underlying machine learning allows it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space. Therefore, those working in this field need to be aware of statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques.

glossary.definition: explicitly programmed by their developers; a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task; able to provide an output only to the inputs that are known; if the algorithm receives an unknown input, it gets into error; usually not a practical choice if a problem under consideration is complex when it is impossible to create a complete list of all possible conditions.
glossary.sentence: The conventional algorithms of computer science are explicitly programmed by their developers. It means that the developers code each step of an algorithm in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs. Therefore, a conventional algorithm is a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task. Such an algorithm is able to provide an output only to the inputs that are known. As a result, if the algorithm receives an unknown input, it gets into error.

glossary.definition: not based on a precise set of instructions; developers provide only general guidelines about a mathematical model that should be used and set initial configuration variables (hyperparameters) of the model; perform mathematical operations to the given data according to the model specified with the aim of finding the best values of parameters to learn a prediction, solve a classification task or find clusters of data; able to provide an output even if the data given are incomplete.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning algorithms are not based on a precise set of instructions. On the contrary, the developers provide only general guidelines about a mathematical model that should be used and set initial configuration variables (hyperparameters) of the model. However, after that, a machine learning algorithm performs mathematical operations to the given data according to the model specified with the aim of finding the best values of parameters to learn a prediction, solve a classification task or find clusters of data.

glossary.definition: Parameters such as the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used in artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.term: computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X
glossary.definition: Very high (Geron, 2017). Additional context: calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high (Geron, 2017). Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point.

glossary.definition: A mechanism used to find the convergence point by calculating values for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset. Calculating it this way is considered inefficient.
glossary.sentence: Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.).

glossary.definition: An optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. It is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables. The gradient descent technique allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables. In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector. The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression. It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables (values of data object features) and a single output variable (a prediction). Thus, it uses the linear function. From the given training dataset, it learns the values of weights used in the linear function. The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: Summary of the main characteristics of the linear regression: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression. It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables (values of data object features) and a single output variable (a prediction). Thus, it uses the linear function. From the given training dataset, it learns the values of weights used in the linear function. The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: In Linear regression, the changing output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent technique allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. Logistic regression also has a cost function which should also be minimised.
glossary.sentence: In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset... The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. Logistic regression has the following cost function , which should also be minimised:

glossary.definition: A positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1, determining how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.
glossary.sentence: A learning rate is one of the hyperparameters used in artificial neural networks. It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.

glossary.definition: a type of learning where the final output received by the artificial neural network can be compared with the desired (actual label) output to apply a learning rule if there is a difference.
glossary.sentence: In the case of supervised machine learning, after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which ... can be compared with the desired (actual label) output.

glossary.definition: A task that generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs.
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs.

glossary.definition: When there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).

glossary.definition: When there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. It is an extension of binary classification.
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification.

glossary.definition: Used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. It predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to. For example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss. Logistic regression also receives as input data the values of the features of data objects, but outputs a binary class label of this object as a result.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to... Logistic regression also receives as input data the values of the features of data objects, but outputs a binary class label of this object as a result.

glossary.definition: A function applied after receiving the linear function's value in logistic regression. It squashes the value of the linear function within the range of [0,1]. The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.
glossary.sentence: Still, after receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function. The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.

glossary.definition: A very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks. The idea based on the classification task involves identifying class labels for new data objects based on a dataset including data objects of two classes.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks. Let’s define the idea of this algorithm based on the classification task. Figure 11 demonstrates the idea of the kNN algorithm. Assume we have a dataset that includes data objects of two classes labelled as suns and clouds. The algorithm should identify class labels for two new data objects represented in yellow.

glossary.definition: A metric used to evaluate the algorithm's performance, which accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: A parameter used to apply gradient descent, also called learning rate. It represents a rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function.
glossary.sentence: To apply the gradient descent, a hyperparameter α called learning rate is used. It represents a rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function.

glossary.definition: A calculation performed with respect to all independent variables as part of the gradient descent technique.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables.

glossary.definition: Independent variables presented in Linear regression.
glossary.sentence: In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector.

glossary.definition: A value usually set in the range [-0.5; 0.5 ] during initial steps of learning.
glossary.sentence: the threshold value usually is set in the range [-0.5; 0.5 ];

glossary.definition: a process in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering, starting with attribute each data object to a single cluster and taking two data objects/clusters with the smallest dissimilarity to make one cluster until only one cluster is acquired.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering. The steps of the agglomerative hierarchical clustering can be described as follows (Figure 3): attribute each data object to a single cluster. This step results in N clusters, where N is the number of data objects in the dataset; take the two data objects with the smallest dissimilarity and make them one cluster. This step results in N-1 clusters; take the two clusters with the smallest dissimilarity and make them one cluster. This step results in the reduction of the number of clusters by 1; repeat Step 3 until only one cluster is acquired.

glossary.definition: applies a top-down clustering approach, starting with a cluster with all data objects inside it and recursively splitting one of the existing clusters into two new clusters at each hierarchy level to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity until each data object makes a cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters. The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) . Finally, the clustering stops when each data object makes a cluster.

glossary.definition: methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: an output of hierarchical clustering that is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset, providing a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format and insight into the way how clusters were formed.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017) , “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format” . Thus, it provides insight into the way how clusters were formed.

glossary.definition: components of a dendrogram that represent merging of data objects into clusters, each having its height.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: terminal points of clades in a dendrogram corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: a horizontal cut- off line usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm, where the number of vertical lines intersected by it represents the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm. The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: a metric for regression tasks that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects, providing positive non-zero values.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.

glossary.definition: an algorithm where the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges that does not demand to specify the number of clusters before the algorithm operation, but requires the developer to decide where to cut the dendrogram and which method of linkage to use.
glossary.sentence: Summarising the information about the hierarchical clustering given in this topic, it is worth mentioning that the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges (Hastie, 2017) . This algorithm does not demand to specify the number of clusters before the algorithm operation. Still, the developer needs to decide a) where to cut the dendrogram to receive the final number of clusters and b) which method of linkage to use to measure similarity between clusters.

glossary.definition: used to compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters, depending on a task whether it is classification, regression, or clustering.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters. The metrics depend on a task, whether it is classification, regression, or clustering.

glossary.definition: a supervised machine learning task that demands making predictions in a continuous range with real value output, where actual labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted labels.
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range. Thus, its output is a real value. Since the regression is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted labels.

glossary.definition: Units resulting from squaring the quantity being estimated, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, the error will represent 'the squared number of persons'; if it predicts the house prices in EUR, then the error value will represent 'squared EUR'.
glossary.sentence: This metric has the same units of measurement as the quantity being estimated. Still, they are squared units; for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, the error will represent "the squared number of persons"; if it predicts the house prices in EUR, then the error value will represent "squared EUR".

glossary.definition: A type of error that is prone to outliers where the smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is. It involves squared units.
glossary.sentence: This type of error is prone to outliers. The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.

glossary.definition: An error measure that applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent "the number of persons"; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent "price in EUR".

glossary.definition: The average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: Also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Values that lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit.
glossary.sentence: The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit.

glossary.definition: Variance in the actual output values which is explained by the features of the constructed model.
glossary.sentence: It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model, for example, if the R2 coefficient of a model is 0.88, then approximately 88% of the observed variation in the actual output values can be explained by the model's features.

glossary.definition: Techniques used across classes in multiclass classification to evaluate performance, for example, macro or weighted averaging.
glossary.sentence: usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging.

glossary.definition: An approach where the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.
glossary.sentence: The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: A data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: A data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: A data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).

glossary.definition: A data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category. It represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category.

glossary.definition: Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class.
glossary.sentence: Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class

glossary.definition: Each column in a confusion matrix represents a predicted class.
glossary.sentence: while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category).
glossary.sentence: In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category).

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: A situation where classes are imbalanced (e.g., 95 labelled as belonging to Class 0, and 5 labelled as Class 1), which makes accuracy not a clear indicator of the performance.
glossary.sentence: However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced.

glossary.definition: A poorly built model which always predicts Class 0 in an example where 95 data objects belong to Class 0 and 5 to Class 1.
glossary.sentence: "In this case, a poorly built "dummy" model which always predicts Class 0, achieves a 95% Accuracy, which indicates a very strong model.

glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives; the accuracy of the positive predictions; the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive; calculated by taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives (in multiclass classification); macro average precision for the task is calculated by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: A concept closely related to artificial neural networks that is widely known nowadays.
glossary.sentence: Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning task that artificial neural networks can perform alongside supervised tasks.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.”

glossary.definition: Cells in the human brain that have a cell body (soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions: many dendrites for receiving signals and a single axon which splits into branches terminating in endbulbs close to other neurons' dendrites; they fire electrical impulses based on synaptic activity.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon... A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse... Thus, the primary activity of a neuron is to fire an electrical impulse along its axon.

glossary.definition: The cell body of a biological neuron containing a nucleus.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon...

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions of a biological neuron that look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites... Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A single root-like extension from the cell body of a biological neuron that eventually splits into branches, each terminating in an endbulb close to another neuron's dendrites; it transmits signals along its length.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon... The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or cell body of another neuron; it generates electrical signals when reached by signals from sending neurons.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron. When the signal from the sending neuron reaches the synapse, it generates an electrical signal in the dendrites of the receiving neuron.

glossary.definition: Synapses that promote firing of neurons and are considered as having a positive weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight...

glossary.definition: Synapses that inhibit firing of neurons and are considered as having a negative weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight...

glossary.definition: A value representing the strength of a synapse, calculated by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight; it can be positive (excitatory) or negative (inhibitory).
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight).

glossary.definition: The primary activity in human mental activity consisting of networks of neurons.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons.

glossary.definition: Associated with synapses where synaptic conductivity increases with electrical current and usage, leading to higher weights for frequently used synapses, while infrequently used synapses may die off due to decreasing conductivity.
glossary.sentence: Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses. It is known that a synapse is made stronger by the however much more electrical current it must carry... Synapses that are used often obtain an increasingly higher weight... This can even lead to them dying off

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of the neuron defined in 1943, consisting of inputs, weights, output, combination function, activation function, and bias input, operating based on principles similar to biological neurons.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron... In general, an artificial neuron has: one or more inputs... real-valued weights... an output... a function for combining input signals... a transfer (activation) function... a bias input

glossary.definition: Numerical values representing data object features in a dataset that enter the artificial neuron from the environment or other neurons, typically labelled by indexed x.
glossary.sentence: one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset.

glossary.definition: values that modify input values at each layer of the network.
glossary.sentence: input values are modified by the weights at each layer of the network.

glossary.definition: A numerical value representing the final solution of an artificial neural network or signal sent to other neurons, typically indexed y.
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons. It is usually represented by indexed y.

glossary.definition: A function for combining input signals (typically summation) to provide a single numerical value, though other functions like product or max/min are allowed.
glossary.sentence: a function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value. It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs.

glossary.definition: Also known as activation function; it determines the output of the neuron and allows modeling complex non-linear relationships, with common choices including step, sign, linear, and sigmoid functions.
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships... many activation functions have been tested, but only a few have found practical applications; the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: An input added at every layer that is included in the weighted sum calculation by each processing unit.
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input. Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: also called hard limit functions, often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks (Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: also called the step and sign activation functions, often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks (Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: neurons that use the step and sign activation functions (also called hard limit functions) for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a task that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs

glossary.definition: a task which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data

glossary.definition: comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: single neurons in artificial neural networks, also called nodes.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: a function for combining input signals used by every neuron to receive an output that is sent to other neurons again.
glossary.sentence: Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.

glossary.definition: a function for calculating an output signal applied by every neuron in artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again. ... a function for calculating an output signal (the activation function)

glossary.definition: the way numerical values are propagated through connections of neurons when outputs become inputs for other neurons.
glossary.sentence: In such a way, numerical values are propagated through connections of neurons.

glossary.definition: a typical artificial neural network comprises a hierarchy of layers, and the neurons on the networks are arranged along with these layers.
glossary.sentence: A typical artificial neural network comprises a hierarchy of layers, and the neurons on the networks are arranged along with these layers.

glossary.definition: A pre-defined layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons;

glossary.definition: A pre-defined layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;

glossary.definition: layers where error is calculated differently than for output neurons and used only for neurons in these layers.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two types of errors: the first is used for output neurons, and the second is used only for neurons in hidden layers.

glossary.definition: neurons that contain summation and activation functions, found in the output and hidden layers but not in the input layer.
glossary.sentence: On the other hand, the output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions. ... The input layer does not contain computing neurons because it only receives inputs signals from the environment and passes them to the hidden layers.

glossary.definition: the characteristic of artificial neural networks where all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks have a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a type of artificial neural network that has connections only in one direction, where every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units with no loops.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops. Thus, a feed-forward network represents a function of its current input.

glossary.definition: a type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs, forming a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: a method applied to modify the weights of the network when there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network.
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: also called graceful degradation; it is the resistance to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little because knowledge of the network is distributed all over the network.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: also called fault tolerance; explains why network operation changes very little when processing units or connections are damaged because knowledge of the network is distributed.
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: projecting probable consequences of given situations
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems... Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: a task or finding the "best" organization of constraints.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints

glossary.definition: a task or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal

glossary.definition: a task determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables

glossary.definition: A system that learns the best possible values for weights that will allow correctly to classify the data.
glossary.sentence: The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights that will allow correctly to classify the data (Jones, 2009). The learning is based on the small adjustments of weights after each training example to reduce the difference between the actual and desired output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the simplest and commonly known type of artificial neural network that can learn only linearly separable functions.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: functions that a Single Layer Perceptron can learn.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: An input provided with its real classification label (the real output) that is given to the perceptron.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron;

glossary.definition: The real classification label of a training example.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron;

glossary.definition: The output received from the perceptron after it processes an input based on summation and activation functions available in the processing unit.
glossary.sentence: after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output;

glossary.definition: A function available in the processing unit that is used by the perceptron to process inputs.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit;

glossary.definition: A condition that stops the learning process, such as when the total classification error reaches a pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron continues the learning process (Steps 2-5) for other examples in the training set until some termination criterion is reached. The learning can be stopped if the total classification error on all training examples reaches the pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved.

glossary.definition: The sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: A method usually applied to compute the weight correction.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule :

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: Another hyperparameter is the number of epochs . An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of network that has greater processing power and can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A layer consisting of computing neurons, usually performing the detection of features. Neurons in this layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.

glossary.definition: A task that allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.
glossary.sentence: The binary classification task allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.

glossary.definition: A phase where inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: A phase involving propagation of errors backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: A task where the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: units that do not have specified values in training data and are responsible for some fraction of the error in output units.
glossary.sentence: the training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units.

glossary.definition: neurons for which the predicted output and real class label are known, allowing calculation of error.
glossary.sentence: In the output neuron, we know the predicted output and the real class label and, thus, can calculate the error in the following way:

glossary.definition: the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems; an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system.
glossary.sentence: In general, 'knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems'... It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system.

glossary.definition: a memory that has unlimited capacity and provides long-term storage of knowledge gained in different ways.
glossary.sentence: Humans gain knowledge in different ways and keep it in the long-term memory. This memory has an unlimited capacity and provides a long-term storage of knowledge.

glossary.definition: an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.

glossary.definition: a component into which knowledge is added to an intelligent computer system; not all intelligent systems need a knowledge base because some can be developed using weak methods
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence. Thus not all intelligent systems need the knowledge base because intelligent computer systems can also be developed using weak methods...

glossary.definition: a component of a knowledge-based intelligent system that allows the system to use knowledge stored in the knowledge base; its aim is to interpret and evaluate facts with the aim of supporting problem-solving, answering user’s questions or building an action plan.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine allows the system to use knowledge stored in the knowledge base. Its aim is to interpret and evaluate facts stored in the knowledge base with the aim of supporting problem-solving, answering user's questions or building an action plan.

glossary.definition: a method used by the inference engine to describe knowledge; typically separated from the knowledge base to allow adding new knowledge, modifying, or removing existent knowledge.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge. Typically, the inference engine is separated from the knowledge base and this allows to add easily new knowledge, as well as to modify and remove existent knowledge from the knowledge base either during the development stage of the intelligent system or during the system's lifetime.

glossary.definition: a storage that stores unrelated facts, for example purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base; works in close collaboration with a domain expert but is not a specialist in all problem domains
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains.

glossary.definition: anyone who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain; possesses domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows them to solve problems in a more effective way
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain... Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base; includes tools for analysis, generation of knowledge, checking consistency, and can offer user interfaces like diagrams or structured descriptions
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces... Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.

glossary.definition: intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance; a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems... An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts...

glossary.definition: forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems... Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems... Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems... Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems... Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems... Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour...

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems... Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge; several categories exist including logical, networked, structured, and procedural/rule-based schemas
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence...

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge
glossary.sentence: ...logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge...

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; based on associationist theories of human mind
glossary.sentence: ...networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them...

glossary.definition: use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/concept
glossary.sentence: ...structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept...

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions
glossary.sentence: ...procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind... These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts...

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them; a knowledge base rooted in semantic networks includes a network of concepts and their relations
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them... Therefore, a knowledge base rooted in semantic networks includes a network of concepts and their relations.

glossary.definition: A natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”.
